properties.description:
***
📝 [Internal S&D Documentation](https://ufgat.sharepoint.com/:w:/r/sites/OG-Summersemester20242/Freigegebene%20Dokumente/General/Academic%20Year%202025-26/BA_StadtLaborLinz_Frankviertel/SOCIAL%20RELATIONS/Social%20groups/9.%20ATELIER%20TRANSPART.docx?d=wdd0289586e53493d95f8ff65a70f709a&csf=1&web=1&e=rBoNL6)
***
📍 [Spacial implications map of Kiosk]
***

Atelier Transpart is a place for artists and a place that has found its own way through the history of the Franckviertel. Founded in 2016 by Lama Ghanem, the atelier has its roots in an earlier project from 2009, when Linz was European Capital of Culture. At that time, a Pixel Hotel with separate rooms along the Danube operated at the edges of the institutional cultural scene. 

Today, Atelier Transpart sits at Brunnenplatz, right at the heart of the network that has grown up around the Kiosk. The idea is exchange: not only artistic, but transnational. Lama Ghanem understands the atelier as a bridge between political and cultural content, between local initiatives and international artists. 

The atelier offers studio residencies; the application process is currently being opened via a new website, allowing artists to apply directly. Planned are more workshops, a stronger presence in the public space around Brunnenplatz, and eventually a three-day art market. An earlier format, the Cafe and Draw, a low-threshold public drawing gathering, is expected to resume in spring. 

The project "What's in my Suitcase?" embedded the atelier in the neighbourhood and built collaborations with other local initiatives. Artist and Kiosk co-organiser Bernhard Hummer was also part of this network. 

Structurally, Atelier Transpart faces challenges common to many small cultural initiatives: limited funding, low visibility in the cityscape, and difficulty planning with long-term security. The proximity to the Kiosk network and the planned opening to more cooperations are attempts to break this isolation.
properties.title: Atelier Transpart

properties.description:
***
📝 [Internal S&D Documentation](https://ufgat.sharepoint.com/:w:/r/sites/OG-Summersemester20242/Freigegebene%20Dokumente/General/Academic%20Year%202025-26/BA_StadtLaborLinz_Frankviertel/SOCIAL%20RELATIONS/Social%20groups/04.%20DON%20BOSCO.docx?d=w97cb360253ab4ed092e11419b9a0daeb&csf=1&web=1&e=7iil0T)
***
📍 [Spacial implications map of Kiosk]
***
Don Bosco is a parish without a priest. Since the Salesians left, the community has been led by a volunteer team: men and women who receive no salary and take on responsibility because they choose to. This is not taken for granted, and it works. 

The parish, built in 1927, sits in a neighbourhood where around 80 to 85 percent of residents have a migration background. Rather than asking how to bring more people into the church, Don Bosco asked the opposite qüstion: what do people actually need, and how do we go to where they are? A social worker, Karin Roller-Roprecht, was brought in to find out. Her findings: the church is wanted, social need is significant, and what is missing above all is a consumption-free space to meet. 

Since then, Don Bosco has worked by five principles of social-space orientation: start from people's lived reality, identify their resources, network, go out, and be welcoming. In practice, this means the Lenten bread is not distributed inside the church. It is carried out into the neighbourhood on Ash Wednesday, including during Ramadan evenings. A dog training area gets a dog blessing. A school class runs a flea market. The ground floor of the church building has been renovated as a youth centre and opens every Friday. 

For children from socially disadvantaged families, Ursula Zettler offers homework support, followed by play, baking and laughter. Education and safety, joined together. The Romanian Orthodox community holds its own services in the same building. That was not an easy step, but it was taken. 

Don Bosco has been part of the Kiosk network from the very beginning and understands itself as a church that dös not wait.
properties.title: Don Bosco

properties.description:
***
📝 [Internal S&D Documentation](https://ufgat.sharepoint.com/:w:/r/sites/OG-Summersemester20242/Freigegebene%20Dokumente/General/Academic%20Year%202025-26/BA_StadtLaborLinz_Frankviertel/SOCIAL%20RELATIONS/Social%20groups/8.%20FRANCK%20KISTL%20(1).docx?d=w053c500edb29481fa1b156108ea234da&csf=1&web=1&e=N2qlls)
***
📍 [Spacial implications map of Kiosk](https://ufgat.sharepoint.com/sites/OG-Summersemester20242/Freigegebene%20Dokumente/Forms/AllItems.aspx?viewid=051ab864%2D6ff3%2D41c3%2D8932%2D13e8bf4a54eb&id=%2Fsites%2FOG%2DSummersemester20242%2FFreigegebene%20Dokumente%2FGeneral%2FAcademic%20Year%202025%2D26%2FBA%5FStadtLaborLinz%5FFrankviertel%2FSOCIAL%20RELATIONS%2FSpatial%20Implications%2FFRANKISTL%2F2nd%20Trial%2FTECNICO%20ORIZZ%2Epdf&parent=%2Fsites%2FOG%2DSummersemester20242%2FFreigegebene%20Dokumente%2FGeneral%2FAcademic%20Year%202025%2D26%2FBA%5FStadtLaborLinz%5FFrankviertel%2FSOCIAL%20RELATIONS%2FSpatial%20Implications%2FFRANKISTL%2F2nd%20Trial)
***

Franck Kistl is a food cooperative, and a rather unusual one. Founded in 2014 by a small group, it has grown into a network of around 100 members and 30 to 40 regional organic farmers, of whom ten to fifteen supply regularly. The principle is simple: members order collectively, the cooperative collects the goods and passes them on without any markup. What the farmer charges is what the member pays. 

This is not standard practice. Most intermediary stages between producer and consumer are points at which a margin is extracted, often at the expense of the smallest farms. Franck Kistl eliminates these stages. Transport costs are shared; otherwise, the price stays with the producer. Many of the participating farms are too small for official organic certification, but not too small for the Kistl. 

Membership costs ten euros per month, which gös directly towards rent paid to GWG, the city housing company. Orders can be collected weekly from the stockroom. Anne Janssen is one of seven people responsible for running it, linking the Kistl to the wider neighbourhood network. 

Plans are underway to hold public events four times a year. A mobile kitchen unit on wheels is also being considered, something that could be used for open-air events and shared with the Kiosk garden or the Nachbarschaftscafe. 

Organic food is expensive; the Kistl knows this. But collective purchasing makes many things accessible that would otherwise be out of reach. And the connection to small local producers is more than pragmatic. It is a statement about whose work deserves to be made visible.
properties.title: Franckkistl

properties.description:
***
📝 [Internal S&D Documentation](https://ufgat.sharepoint.com/:w:/r/sites/OG-Summersemester20242/Freigegebene%20Dokumente/General/Academic%20Year%202025-26/BA_StadtLaborLinz_Frankviertel/SOCIAL%20RELATIONS/Social%20groups/11.%20JUGENDZENTRUM%20%20FRANX.docx?d=w2830481069f7429c99f37c9904a3814e&csf=1&web=1&e=4I4IHC)
***
📍 [Spacial implications map of Kiosk]
***
In autumn 2025, FRANX relocated to new premises at Franckstrasse 10, directly at the bus stop, with a significantly expanded offer. Alongside the youth club for 12 to 18 year olds, there is now a TEENIEclub for 8 to 12 year olds. 

The club room offers table football, billiards, gaming consoles, a bar and seating areas: a place to talk, play and hang out. In the basement, there is a project room for workshops, crafts and creative initiative. A recording studio, currently being renovated, will soon offer free recording and mixing sessions with real equipment and real software. 

FRANX is a facility of the Verein Jugend und Freizeit (VJF), which runs several youth centres in Linz. The work follows the principle of open youth work: voluntary, free of charge, anonymous, low-threshold. No one has to fill in a form, no one needs a reason to come. You can simply be there. 

There is also a counselling offer for young people between 12 and 18, anonymous and confidential. Social workers are present and actively go out into the district: into schools, into public squares, into the neighbourhood. This is called Streetwork, and it means: we come to you, you do not have to come to us. 

FRANX also has a dedicated offer for girls and young women: fixed times when the club is open exclusively for them. Gender-sensitive youth work is not an add-on at VJF; it is a guiding principle. In a district where many young people know cramped conditions and have little room to unfold, FRANX is more than a leisure offer. It is territory.
properties.title: Jugendzentrum FRANX

properties.description:
***
# Was ist der Kiosk?
The Kiosk is a 1950s pavilion in Stiegelbauernstraße in the Franckviertel and since 2019 the focal point of a growing neighbourhood initiative. When the last tobacco shop owner announced his retirement and warned that the building would likely be demolished after he left, the community decided: not if we can help it. 

From that moment, a broad network of associations, artists, social workers and residents came together to occupy and shape the building's future. In 2020, the community garden behind the Kiosk took root: born of a gürrilla planting action the city tried to stop, now flourishing with raised beds, a fig tree and tomatös. The Nachbarschaftscafe has gathered under the covered roof every Tüsday since. Art and culture projects, funded through two city grants (Bingo Bingo 2021 and Bingo Go Go 2022), animated the display windows and the surrounding park. 

A turning point came in November 2022, when the building received heritage protection. This recognised its architectural significance as one of the last 1950s pavilions of its kind in Linz. Paradoxically, this protection preserves precisely the features that would make commercial exploitation difficult: old windows, historic doors, original tiles. For the initiative, this is a strategic advantage. 

The goal remains: the Kiosk as a permanently community-managed cultural centre for the Franckviertel. The mayor has announced renovation plans, though costs have risen from 250,000 euros in 2019 to around 700,000 euros today. The initiative has no key of its own; that still sits with the city. But the community has its foot in the door, and no wrecking ball will dislodge it. 

The initiative is coordinated by Anne Janssen. Artist Bernhard Hummer has been involved since 2020.
properties.title: Kiosk

properties.description:
***
📝 [Internal S&D Documentation](https://ufgat.sharepoint.com/:w:/r/sites/OG-Summersemester20242/Freigegebene%20Dokumente/General/Academic%20Year%202025-26/BA_StadtLaborLinz_Frankviertel/SOCIAL%20RELATIONS/Social%20groups/03.%20NACHBARCAFE.docx?d=w9b5f6b4afe9a46e8a75f9a02bd299ac8&csf=1&web=1&e=KhSGPc)
***
The Nachbarschaftscafe is as simple as it is indispensable: every Tüsday, residents of the Franckviertel gather under the roof of the Kiosk pavilion for coffee, home-baked cake and conversation. No minimum spend, no programme. Just a place to come together. 

The initiative grew alongside the Kiosk project around 2019, when it became clear that the neighbourhood lacked a consumption-free meeting space. This finding also appeared in the social-space analysis conducted by Don Bosco parish. The organisation PROGES took over premises at Stieglbaürnstrasse 11 for community use, and the cafe found its home there. Karin has since taken on responsibility for running it: someone who discovered the initiative through the Kiosk garden project, and for whom the cafe is far more than a meeting point. It helped her reconnect with community after depression. 

At the start of 2026, the cafe faces an existential qüstion. The rental contract with GWG, the city housing association, has expired, and the future of the space is uncertain. With no institutional backing to fall back on, the cafe survives on personal commitment, occasional donations and self-financing events like the Christmas gathering, where Karin offers home-baked biscuits and warm punch. 

One possible solution lies in the conversion of the Kiosk into an official community centre, which the mayor has promised but not yet delivered. In the meantime, the Nachbarschaftscafe remains what it has always been: a living proof that the simplest ideas are often the most necessary ones. And that community needs no programme, only a place and people willing to show up.
properties.title: Nachbarschafts Café

properties.description:
***
📝 [Internal S&D Documentation](https://ufgat.sharepoint.com/:w:/r/sites/OG-Summersemester20242/Freigegebene%20Dokumente/General/Academic%20Year%202025-26/BA_StadtLaborLinz_Frankviertel/SOCIAL%20RELATIONS/Social%20groups/12.%20ProMente.docx?d=wa1228932564e4c77a65f2dc7d29a22b7&csf=1&web=1&e=Ho5t9v)
***
📍 [Spacial implications map of Kiosk]
***
It’s one of the biggest organisations which supports people with mental and social difficulties in Upper Austria. It was created in 1964 and got the name “pro mente infirmis”. They were created to protect the rights of people with mental issues. 

In 1967 was opened the first consulting and supportive services 

In 1970-1980 was invented first addiction prevention programs and support of youth.

In the 1990s the organisation started helping with work integration. They created the organisation called “Arbeitsassistenz” and programs which help people with mental disorders to find employment. 

Over the last 30 years Pro Mente OÖ became the biggest professional support system providing psychotherapy, consultations, mobile assistance, help with finding housing, prevention of addiction, support of youth and labor integration programs. 

They get funding through subsidies, partnerships with government agencies and other sources such as Land OberÖsterreich, AMS, etc.
properties.title: pro mente

properties.description:
***
📝 [Internal S&D Documentation](https://ufgat.sharepoint.com/:w:/r/sites/OG-Summersemester20242/Freigegebene%20Dokumente/General/Academic%20Year%202025-26/BA_StadtLaborLinz_Frankviertel/SOCIAL%20RELATIONS/Social%20groups/02.%20SCHLOT.docx?d=w2a84cebfd91d49f28503a797ec71184c&csf=1&web=1&e=GxvyZR)

***
📍 [Spacial implications map of Kiosk]
***
About ten or eleven years ago, three artists and two musicians founded the association in a former industrial hall on the site of what was once the Fehrer mattress factory in the Franckviertel. What they built, they built themselves: walls, stage, rehearsal room. 

Today, Schlot houses around ten artists with their own studios and four to five bands who use the rehearsal space regularly. Four to five times a year, there are public concerts, exhibitions and performances. Once a year comes the Schlot Christmas market: not a regular Christkindlmarkt, but a persiflage of one, complete with a shooting gallery, esoteric stall, dubious Christmas trees and friendship bracelets with messages that make you stop and think. 

Funding comes from studio and rehearsal room rents, supplemented by city and state grants. A three-year funding period is ending soon and a new application is pending. A studio space costs 150 euros per month, which is only possible, members acknowledge, because public money covers the rest. 

Schlot occupies an industrial building, and deliberately so. Noise regulations in former industrial zones are more permissive than in residential areas; the acoustics of the self-built rehearsal room, with its deliberately non-right-angled walls, are excellent. Bands can play late into the night. This is not coincidence, it is strategy. 

Collaboration with the Kiosk network is ongoing: Schlot lends materials, contributes to display window exhibitions and has staged two joint performances, including the legendary Bingo Bingo event that the neighbourhood has not forgotten. A clear example of what happens when art leaves its bubble and connects with the place where it lives.
properties.title: Schlot

properties.description:
***
📝 [Internal S&D Documentation](https://ufgat.sharepoint.com/:w:/r/sites/OG-Summersemester20242/Freigegebene%20Dokumente/General/Academic%20Year%202025-26/BA_StadtLaborLinz_Frankviertel/SOCIAL%20RELATIONS/Social%20groups/07.%20Stadtteilzentrum.docx?d=w44d2055e6e074e69805f69c90e13f27b&csf=1&web=1&e=p4I7QH)
***
The Stadtteilzentrum Franckviertel / Bulgariplatz is a welfare-oriented community work centre run by the City of Linz, and in the most straightforward terms a point of contact for anyone who has any kind of qüstion: about their dog, about money, about their children, about a space they need. This sounds mundane; it is not. 

Tom Dautovic, who has worked there since 2020, describes the Franckviertel with a key observation: it used to feel like a village in the city. The weekly market at Lonsdorferplatz is gone, the Salesian parish presence has gone, the only ATM is inside a shop that closes on Sundays. The Franckviertel has become a sleeping district, without a centre, without a shared place. People identify as Franckviertler:innen, not as Linzers. But where do they actually meet? 

The Stadtteilzentrum tries to fill this gap through networking, information, activation and concrete projects. The most visible shared format is the Week of the Neighbourhood, now in its fifth year, which deliberately places events across both sub-districts to build bridges across the railway line that separates Franckviertel from Bulgariplatz. 

Dautovic describes the gentrification of the district without euphemism: first workers, then migrants, now students and artists. The classic pattern. Cheap, neglected railway housing is being demolished; new buildings with private investors are going up. Former residents are displaced, some to other parts of the city. This is called urban development. 

The most pressing qüstion remains the immediate one: where do I get support? Where can I find food? Where can I do something? The Stadtteilzentrum is oriented around these questions and hopes, in dü course, to have the key to the renovated Kiosk in hand.
properties.title: Stadtteilzentrum

properties.description:
***
📝 [Internal S&D Documentation](https://ufgat.sharepoint.com/:w:/r/sites/OG-Summersemester20242/Freigegebene%20Dokumente/General/Academic%20Year%202025-26/BA_StadtLaborLinz_Frankviertel/SOCIAL%20RELATIONS/Social%20groups/6.%20STOP%20(1).docx?d=w9267640b22eb427882be9c8d894b707a&csf=1&web=1&e=mNVpqn)
***
📍 [Spacial implications map of Kiosk]
***
STOP stands for Stadtteile ohne Partnergewalt, meaning Neighbourhoods without Partner Violence, and is a prevention project of the Linz Women's Shelter. The concept originates in Hamburg, where Dr. Sabine Stövesand developed it in 2006: domestic violence is not a private matter. It happens in the neighbourhood, and the neighbourhood can do something about it. 

The Women's Shelter provides protection for women and children in acute danger. But far more important would be for that danger not to arise in the first place. That is why STOP dös not work primarily with victims, but with the people around them: the neighbours who hear something, the friends who notice something, the people who do not know what to do. 

In Linz, STOP has been active since summer 2021, in two districts: the Franckviertel and Urfa. Methods are varied: stairwell conversations, women's neighbourhood tables, civil courage workshops, and actions at clubs and in public spaces. Together with a local football club, STOP created the Stopplatz, a public seating area with a visible message. During the 16 Days of Activism against Violence against Women, from 25 November to 10 December, STOP participates in the international Orange the World campaign. 

What distinguishes STOP is a broad understanding of violence: physical harm is only its most visible form. Control, financial dependency, isolation, being made invisible, all of this is violence. And a cycle of relationship violence never begins with a blow. It begins earlier, and often at first it looks like love. 

In the Franckviertel, STOP works closely with the Kiosk network. The trust built up in the community over years is the precondition for people being able to speak about violence at all.
properties.title: STOP

properties.description:
***
📝 [Internal S&D Documentation](https://ufgat.sharepoint.com/:w:/r/sites/OG-Summersemester20242/Freigegebene%20Dokumente/General/Academic%20Year%202025-26/BA_StadtLaborLinz_Frankviertel/SOCIAL%20RELATIONS/Social%20groups/05.%20U%CC%88BER%20DEN%20TELLERRAND.docx?d=w14a09fee11d84be2aa82b130306e26c9&csf=1&web=1&e=p6yYFY)
***
📍 [Spacial implications map of Kiosk]
***
Über den Tellerrand, literally "Beyond the Edge of the Plate", began in Berlin in 2014 with a simple gesture: students set up side tables, lit portable cookers and invited refugees and locals to cook together. The aim was genuine encounter at eye level, beyond the roles of helper and helped. The concept spread; today there are 42 local groups, called satellites, across several countries. In Austria, there is only one: Linz. 

The Linz satellite, co-founded by Beate Adam, celebrates its tenth anniversary next year. What began as a weekend project is now a small catering enterprise with around twelve to fifteen team members. Many have been present since 2016, some now Austrian citizens. The monthly Sunday brunches are fully booked; catering runs almost entirely by word of mouth. über den Tellerrand is entirely self-financed, with no public funding. Fixed costs of 1,500 euros per month are covered by workshops, catering and events. 

Every Friday, in partnership with Volkshilfe, a language cafe runs alongside a free lunch made from rescüd food. Since the rise in living costs, more and more people are coming to these Friday gatherings. 

über den Tellerrand also runs a community garden, sewing workshops, a monthly Generations Cafe bringing together seniors and kindergarten children, and the programme Families at a Table, which encourages Franckviertel families to discover Linz beyond their own district. The name says it all: to look beyond the edge of the plate means looking beyond one's own district, one's own habits, one's own table. 

The premises were redesigned in 2023 by students from the Kunst-Uni Linz with a budget of 1,000 euros, using existing materials and with care.
properties.title: Über den Tellerrand

properties.description:
***
📝 [Internal S&D Documentation](https://ufgat.sharepoint.com/:w:/r/sites/OG-Summersemester20242/Freigegebene%20Dokumente/General/Academic%20Year%202025-26/BA_StadtLaborLinz_Frankviertel/SOCIAL%20RELATIONS/Social%20groups/10.%20VOLKSHAUS%20FRANCKVIERTEL.docx?d=w4ef524af31c3462485cd144a33c6cfff&csf=1&web=1&e=kxsrxj)
***
📍 [Spacial implications map of Kiosk]
***
The Volkshaus Franckviertel is one of the oldest and most enduring public buildings in the district. Designed by architect Kurt Kühne and built in 1929 at the corner of Franckstrasse and Füchselstrasse, originally as a community hall, it was renovated in 1970. The building is listed as a heritage monument; its brick facade, clinker ornamentation and distinctive central rotunda are a visible testament to interwar architecture. 

Today, the Volkshaus is an event venü managed by the City of Linz. It can be hired for concerts, cabaret, theatre, seminars, club gatherings and private celebrations. The programme regularly includes concerts in jazz, pop, rock and cabaret, and cultural events of all kinds. Equipment includes a stage, sound system, large projection screen and full blackout capability. 

Hire costs are graduated by event type and duration, from accessible rates for non-commercial associations to day rates for commercial use. This makes the Volkshaus one of the few places in the Franckviertel that provides infrastructure for larger community events. 

In a district that no longer has a real centre, as Tom Dautovic of the Stadtteilzentrum aptly notes, the Volkshaus fulfils a function that gös beyond venü hire. It is one of the few places where residents of the Franckviertel can gather for shared occasions: for music, for politics (the SPö Franckviertel is also based here) or for celebration. 

The building is also a mirror of the district's history. Built as a community hall in 1929, it has witnessed decades of change: from a workers' neighbourhood through migration histories to the current wave of gentrification. It is still standing.
properties.title: Volkshaus Franckviertel
